coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional GQDs-Concanavalin A@Fe3O4 nanocomposites for cancer cells detection and targeted drug delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Dutta Chowdhury, Ankan
Ganganboina, Akhilesh Babu
Tsai, Yuan-chung
Chiu, Hsin-cheng
Doong, Ruey-an
coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional nanocomposites containing intrinsic property for serving as the sensing elements as well as targeted nanoconjugates are highly preferred in various therapeutic applications. In this work, nanocomposites of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Fe3O4 with conjugation of lectin protein, concanavalin A, to form GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites are developed for both detection of cancer cell and release of drugs to HeLa cells. The GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites deposited on Pt electrode can detect cancerous HeLa cells over normal endothelial cells with a dynamic linear range of 5 × 102 to 1 × 105 cells mL−1 with a detection limit of 273 cell mL−1. The GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 also can serve as nanocarriers for loading and delivering doxorubicin (Dox). The in vitro cell images show that the Dox concentration in HeLa cells is enhanced more than double in the presence of external magnetic field due to the incorporation of Fe3O4 in the nanocarrier. The cytotoxicity assay indicates that the susceptibility of cancerous HeLa cells to Dox is 13% higher than that of normal cells, confirming the selective role of ConA in nanocarriers. Results clearly indicate the GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites as a promising material for cancer cell detection and targeted Dox release toward HeLa cells which can serve as the multifunctional platform for novel cancer cell diagnostic and therapeutic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cancerous cells
Multifunctional nanocomposites
Electrochemical detection
Biosensing
Doxorubicin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQD-ConA and GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites
Characterisations of GQDs and GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites
Specific detection of cancer cells by GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites
Characterization of Dox loaded GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites as nanocarriers
Dox release profile of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers
Magnetic effect of Fe3O4 in the nanocarriers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) particle size distribution of GQDs (C) HRTEM image of single GQD, (D) transparent PBS solution under 365 nm UV light irradiation turns into bright blue when containing GQDs (E) TEM images of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites, (F) HRTEM images of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 from selected area of E and (G) deciphered fringes of Fe3O4 (0.26 nm) and GQD (0.21 nm) presented in the marked area of F. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Deconvoluted XPS N 1s spectra of (A) APTES-coated Fe3O4 and (B) GQD-ConA@Fe3O4, (C) Saturation magnetization of Fe3O4-based nanomaterials at 300 K and (D) TGA curves of the as-prepared Fe3O4, APTES-coated Fe3O4 and GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of Pt||GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 electrodes incubated at various concentrations of cancerous HeLa cells in 10 mM of pH 7.4 PBS buffer solution containing 100 mM NaCl and 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3− /4-, (B) calibration curves of cancer cell measured from oxidation and reduction peak intensities of CV. The CV curves are measured in the potential window of −0.2 V to 0.6 V at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 and (C) comparison of CV peak current of between cancerous (HeLa and MCF-7) and noncancerous (Endothelial cells) cells.

(A) The hydrodynamic diameters of GQDs, Fe3O4, GQD@Fe3O4, GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 and Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 by dynamic laser scattering (DLS) and (B) Dox loading of GQDs by monitoring fluorescence spectra of GQDs at 460 nm of emission.

(A) Schematic representation and (B) side (left) and aerial (right) pictures of dialysis chamber to optimize the Dox release at various pHs and (C) cumulative Dox release from Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanoparticles at various pHs (inset is the release percentage of Dox). For both set of experiments at pH 6 and 7.4, Dox is collected from two separated chambers in the presence and absence of external magnetic field after regular time interval.

Fluorescence images of time dependent Dox delivery of Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers in HeLa cells (A) in the presence and (B) absence of external magnetic field. (C) MTT assay of 0.5, 1 and 2 μg mL−1 Dox loaded GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers in HeLa and endothelial cells in the presence and absence of external magnetic field. The data are displayed as means ± standard deviation with n = 5.

Comparative fluorescence images of Dox delivery for (A) Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers and (B) Dox-GQD@Fe3O4 nanocarriers (without ConA) in HeLa and endothelial cells at identical condition (C) MTT assay of HeLa cells, incubated with as-prepared GQDs (20 μg mL−1), GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 without Dox (50 μg mL−1), Dox loaded GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers and only Dox (Dox loadings in both case are 0.5, 1 and 2 μg mL−1) after 2, 8 and 12 h of incubation. (D) Comparative cytotoxicity of nanocarriers with/without ConA in HeLa and endothelial cells. The data are displayed as means ± standard deviation with n = 5.
Schematic representation of the preparation of Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites and its application in cancer cell detection on electrode and pH triggered release of Dox in cancer cells.
Analytical performance of various electrochemical sensors for cancer cell detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: MWCNTs-Pdnano/PTCA = multiwalled carbon nanotube/nano palladium/3,4,9,10-perylene tetracarboxylic acid.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional GQDs-Concanavalin A@Fe3O4 nanocomposites for cancer cells detection and targeted drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Engineering, National Chiao Tung University, 1001 University Road, Hsinchu 30010, Taiwan, ROC

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, 101 Section 2, Kuang-Fu Road, Hsinchu, 30013, Taiwan, ROC

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biomedical Engineering and Environmental Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Environmental Engineering, National Chiao Tung University, 1001 University Road, Hsinchu 30010, Taiwan, ROC.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional nanocomposites containing intrinsic property for serving as the sensing elements as well as targeted nanoconjugates are highly preferred in various therapeutic applications. In this work, nanocomposites of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Fe3O4 with conjugation of lectin protein, concanavalin A, to form GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites are developed for both detection of cancer cell and release of drugs to HeLa cells. The GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites deposited on Pt electrode can detect cancerous HeLa cells over normal endothelial cells with a dynamic linear range of 5 × 102 to 1 × 105 cells mL−1 with a detection limit of 273 cell mL−1. The GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 also can serve as nanocarriers for loading and delivering doxorubicin (Dox). The in vitro cell images show that the Dox concentration in HeLa cells is enhanced more than double in the presence of external magnetic field due to the incorporation of Fe3O4 in the nanocarrier. The cytotoxicity assay indicates that the susceptibility of cancerous HeLa cells to Dox is 13% higher than that of normal cells, confirming the selective role of ConA in nanocarriers. Results clearly indicate the GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites as a promising material for cancer cell detection and targeted Dox release toward HeLa cells which can serve as the multifunctional platform for novel cancer cell diagnostic and therapeutic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The most challenging goal in early cancer diagnosis by nanotechnology is the development of multifunctional nanoparticles which can simultaneously serve as sensitive and cell-specific recognizers as well as targeted tumour treatment [1]. Extensive efforts have been devoted to design such kind of nanocomposites that can combine early stage cancer cell detection ability with efficient imaging, drug storage, and controllable drug release capabilities. Recently, inorganic as well as graphitic quantum dots have been emerged as promising materials for this purpose due to their advantages of controllable and enhanced endocytosis, surface enhanced cargo binding activity and easy introduction of multi-functionalities to imaging and therapeutic applications [2–7]. To introduce their target specificity with magnetic and recyclable properties, magnetic iron oxide (Fe3O4) nanoparticles have currently been recognized as one of the most promising and well explored materials [8–11]. The successful combination of nanoparticles represents an exciting advancement in the field of nanomedicine, which is ideal to engineer “smart” multifunctional nanoparticles that are capable of performing these tasks in a wide variety of applications ranging from sensing to drug delivery. The DNA modified Fe3O4@Au magnetic nanoparticles have been synthesized to serve as a selective probe for detection of heavy metal ions [12]. Similarly, the advantage of magnetic behavior of Fe3O4 nanoparticles makes the iron oxide based nanocomposites such as Fe3O4@Pt, Fe3O4@carbon dots and Fe3O4@Au promising materials for different biomedical applications including protein separation [13], drug delivery [14] and sensing [15]. Despite the significant progress in nanotechnology, there is still an essential unmet need to develop novel nanocomposites for simultaneous cancer cell sensing as well as for providing effective target-oriented drug delivery.

Selective recognition of cancer cells over normal healthy cells is the primary and utmost challenging goal in anticancer therapeutics [16–18]. Several bio-recognizable markers have been used to increase the specificity by complementary binding with cell membrane [19–21]. In addition, effective materials including organic complexes [22], folic acid [4] and biomarkers [23] have been already well-established as anchored species with nanocarriers like Au nanoparticles [24], quantum dots [2] and solid lipid nanoparticles [20] to enhance the target specificity [6,25]. More recently, lectin proteins like homotetrameric concanavalin A (ConA), with a size of approximately 8 nm at pH above 5.5, has been in limelight because of its interaction with specific carbohydrate structures on cancer cell surface [26]. ConA can bind primarily to the α-d-mannopyranosides with high specificity in the presence of Ca2+ and Mn2+ [27]. Since the enhanced carbohydrate level is one of the key factors to identify the cancer cell surface, lectin bound nanomaterials have recently been emerged as the useful entities for bio-imaging and various biomedical applications [28]. This unique characteristic gives a great impetus to conjugate ConA with GQDs to enhance the specificity of nanocomposites towards cancerous cells containing high carbohydrate residues with respect to normal cells for the application in the field of electrochemical sensing to drug delivery.

Non-antibody based electrochemical methods have attracted considerable attention for the development of sensing platforms for cancer cells since they are usually performed with simple instrumentation, cost effectiveness and rapid analysis [29]. Recently, an increasing interest has focused on the on the fabrication of simple and inexpensive molecules with effective recognition function to specifically bind with cancer cell. Cancer cells generally have high glycolipids on the surface and can undergo the glycogenation due to the excess glucose oxidation for rapid cell division. The GQD-ConA nanocomposites could serve as a sensing probe for cancer cell detection because the presence of ConA in the nanocomposites can recognize and bind with the glycogeneted environment of cancer cells. In addition, the combination of the nano-dimensional surface of GQDs, magnetic properties of Fe3O4 and cancer cell recognition ability of ConA [22] to fabricate the GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites with pH-triggered controlled release functionality and cell imaging is yet to be followed.

Herein, we have fabricated the multifunctional GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites with therapeutic modality including label free detection of cancer cells, drug storage and controlled release capabilities for the detection of cancer cells and drug delivery of doxorubicin (Dox) to HeLa cells. Scheme 1 shows the preparation of Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites for the cell detection and pH triggered release of Dox to cancer cells. The magnetic Fe3O4 was first coated with APTES and GQD-ConA conjugates were then anchored covalently onto the surface of APTES-Fe3O4 to form GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites. The detection functionality of nanocomposites is initially explored by electrochemical cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the presence of various concentrations of cancerous HeLa cells ranging from 5 × 102 to 1 × 105 cells mL−1. The drug delivery functionality of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 is further carried out by loading Dox as the targeted nanocarriers. In addition, the in vitro release of pH-sensitive Dox was evaluated by the cytotoxicity and cellular uptake of HeLa and endothelial cells simultaneously to clarify their localization and relative toxicity. Results show that the GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites can effectively detect cancer cells in a wide dynamic range of 4 orders of magnitude and the specificity of Dox release toward cancerous cells is significantly enhanced by the attachment of ConA and Fe3O4 nanoparticles, which can open a new avenue to design the pH-sensitive drug delivery carriers.

Magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared using co-precipitation method by adding FeCl3·6H2O and FeSO4·7H2O at the ratio of 2:1 to the aqueous solutions [30]. The aqueous suspension was heated to 60 °C in nitrogen with vigorous stirring. In the later stage, 20 mL of 8 M NH4OH solutions were added dropwise to precipitate the surface-immobilized Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions. Then, the resulting black mixture product was aged at 60 °C for 8 h, separated by a magnet and then washed with deionized water repeatedly. Finally, the synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles were collected from the upper portion of the solution to avoid bigger-sized particles and then dried at 70 °C under vacuum for 12 h. All the solutions were prepared by using bi-distilled deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm) unless otherwise mentioned.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by the pyrolysis method [31]. In brief, 2 g of citric acid (Sigma-Aldrich) was heated to 200 °C until the color of solution changed to orange liquid. The estimated time required for this color change was around 30 min. The orange liquid was added to 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution dropwisely under the vigorously stirred conditions. The as-prepared GQDs were purified by dialysis bag of 1 kDa for 24 h to eliminate un-reacted citric acid. The obtained GQD solution was maintained at pH 7 and kept in refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQD-ConA and GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The ConA (Sigma-Aldrich) loaded GQDs were prepared by physical mixing of 10 mL of 50 μg mL−1 of GQDs with 1 mg of ConA for 1 h at room temperature [32]. To separate the ConA loaded GQDs from un-reacted ConA, GQD-ConA mixture was dialyzed in a 3 kDa dialysis bag for 8 h.

4 mL of APTES and 100 mg of Fe3O4 mixture in dry toluene were refluxed at 120 °C for 20 h under constant stirring to get the APTES-coated Fe3O4 [33]. Then, the precipitate was washed several times with fresh toluene, magnetically separated and then dried at 60 °C under vacuum. The GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites were then synthesized from the as-synthesized ConA loaded GQDs (10 mL of 50 μg mL−1) with 2 mg of APTES coated Fe3O4 nanoparticles via 2 mg N-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) (Sigma-Aldrich) and 3 mg N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) (Alfa Aesar) to undergo EDC/NHS covalent chemistry [34].

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to identify the morphology and size of GQDs and Fe3O4-based nanomaterials by using a JEOL JEM-ARM200F transmission electron microscope and a JEOL JEM-2010 high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) at 200 kV. The crystallinity of Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 was examined by a Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) and operated at a generator voltage and an emission current of 40 kV and 40 mA, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an ESCA Ulvac-PHI 1600 photoelectron spectrometer from Physical Electronics using Al-Kα radiation photon energy at 1486.6 ± 0.2 eV. The thermal properties were examined by thermogravimetric analysis using Mettler Toledo DSC/TGA 3 + Stare system in the presence of N2. The fluorescence spectra of Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 were recorded by Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometers. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 were determined by using a Horiba FT720 spectrophotometer. Magnetic measurements of Fe3O4-based nanomaterials were carried out with a superconducting quantum interference device magnetometer (SQUID MPMS5, Quantum Design Inc.) with a maximum applied continuous field of 10,000 Gauss at room temperature. The hydrodynamic diameters of Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites in aqueous solutions were determined by a Zetasizer Nano ZS dynamic light scattering (DLS) with (Malvern Inst. Ltd., UK).

To determine the specific binding of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites towards cancerous cells, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was carried out in presence of Fe(CN)6 3− /4- on a potentiostat/galvanostat (Autolab) workstation. The platinum (Pt) milli-electrode, used for electrochemical studies, was first cleaned by standard method and then 5 mg of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites was drop-casted on the surface of Pt milli-electrode with PVDF binder and carbon black slurry. After washing with phosphate buffer at pH 7.4, the sensing electrode was stored at 4 °C until use.

To determine the specific binding of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites towards cancerous cells, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was employed to detect HeLa and endothelial cells in 10 mM PBS buffer at pH 7.4 containing 100 mM NaCl and 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3− /4-. CV was carried out on a potentiostat/galvanostat (Autolab) in a conventional three-electrode cell consisting with a Pt milli-electrode (2 mm in diameter), platinum wire, and a saturated Ag/AgCl as working, auxiliary and reference electrodes, respectively. The sensing process was carried out by immersing the sensor electrode into the sample containing various cell concentrations ranging from 5 × 102 to 1 × 105 cells mL−1 for 10 min in order to attach the cells onto ConA-attached electrode surface. The cells-entrapped electrode was then rinsed with PBS buffer solution to remove the loosely bounded cells and then CV curves in the potential window of −0.1 V to 0.6 V at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 were recorded.

To examine the intracellular Dox level in HeLa and endothelial cells, initially cells were incubated in a six wells plate at the concentration of 1 × 106 cells per well at 37 °C in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 for overnight. The cells were treated with free Dox and GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers with different concentrations of Dox loading (0.5, 1 and 2 μg mL−1) for 12 h, and then washed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) twice. To study the effect of magnetic field on the intracellular Dox level, the cells were treated simultaneously with external magnet at 5 T, placing magnet under each well. 1 mL of DMSO was then added into each well for the lysis of cells from the well. For fluorescence microscopy imaging (Olympus, IX70), cells were seeded on 2 cm × 2 cm glass cover slips placed in a six well plate at 1 × 106 cells per well and incubated at 37 °C overnight and followed by the same procedures. Nuclei of cells were not stained with any stainer due to the presence of GQDs in the nanocarriers. GQDs can quench the fluorescence properties of the fluorophore. Therefore, bright field images were taken to identify the location of cells. Finally, the cells were then fixed with mounting gel, and the cover slips were covered with micro-slides to take the images.

Cancerous HeLa and normal endothelial cells were incubated into 96-well plates at a concentration of 1 × 104 cells per well in 0.2 mL of DMEM medium containing 10% FBS and 1% penicillin. The cells were incubated at 37 °C overnight in an atmosphere of 5% CO2. The expended medium was then replaced by fresh medium containing Dox and Dox-loaded nanomaterials at different concentrations (0.5, 1 and 2 μg mL−1). The plates were further incubated at 37 °C for 6 h. After being washed with PBS twice, the 96-well plates were then filled with fresh DMEM (0.2 mL per well) and re-incubated for additional 24 h. To determine the cell viability, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT reagent) was added into the 96-well plate (100 mL per well) and then incubated again at 37 °C for 2 h. The absorbance of each well was determined by the microplate reader at 570 and 600 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisations of GQDs and GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were first synthesized from citric acid via pyrolysis method according to the previous study with minor modification [31]. The TEM image, shown in Fig. 1 A, confirms the homogenous distribution of GQDs where the sizes of GQDs are in the range of 2–10 nm in diameters with a mean size of 5.5 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. 1B). The crystalline structure of a single particle of GQDs is shown in Fig. 1C. The measured lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) lattice plane of defect free graphene [3]. In addition, GQDs show the unique fluorescence property and emit strong blue fluorescence under the 365 nm UV light irradiation (Fig. 1D).

After successful fabrication of GQDs, ConA is attached onto the GQDs surfaces via physical mixing. In addition, the as-prepared Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. S1, see Supplementary data) are coated with APTES by standard salinization process [32] for the preparation of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites. The TEM image of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites shows homogeneous and well-distributed Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 1E), which indicates that the agglomeration of magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles is reduced significantly due to the coating of APTES and GQDs onto the surface. The fringes in HRTEM image clearly show the presence of GQDs along with the Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 1F). The corresponding fringes of GQDs (0.21 nm) [31] and Fe3O4 (0.26 nm) [35] are deciphered in Fig. 1G, confirming the successful formation of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4. Fig. S2 (see Supplementary data) shows the XRD patterns of the as-synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles and Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4. The as-prepared Fe3O4 show sharp peaks centered at 30.12°, 35.39°, 43.04°, 53.58°, 56.93°, and 62.73° 2θ in the XRD patterns, which can be indexed as (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) reflections of the standard Fe3O4 (JCPDS No. 65–3107). After the coating with APTES and then ConA-GQD, the position and intensity of Fe3O4 remains the same, indicating the un-alternation of crystal structure of Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

XPS was used to identify the change in element species before and after the loading of APTES and GQD-ConA. As shown in Fig. S3 (Supplementary data), the survey scan of Fe3O4 shows the standard peaks of O (532 eV) and Fe (710 and 724 eV) [36]. After surface modification of APTES, two additional peaks at C 1s (284 eV) and N 1s (400 eV) are observed in the survey scan of APTES coated Fe3O4 nanocomposites, clearly indicating the introduction of amine group of APTES to Fe3O4 after functionalization [37]. To investigate the nitrogen linkage of APTES in APTES coated Fe3O4 and the GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites, the N 1s peak is further deconvoluted. The deconvoluted N 1s spectra displays a significant change in N-H (399.2 eV) and C-N (401.8 eV) peaks (Fig. 2 A and B). The peak intensity of N-H decreases while C-N increases from APTES-coated Fe3O4 to GQD-ConA@Fe3O4, indicating the formation of amide bond from the reaction of free -NH2 group of APTES with -COOH group of GQDs. The deconvoluted Fe 2p peak (710.9 eV) of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites illustrated in Fig. S4A (Supplementary data) shows Fe2+ (710.7 eV) and Fe3+ peaks (712.7 eV), which are the characteristic peaks of Fe3O4 [36]. In addition, the similar peak position to those of APTES-coated Fe3O4 and as-prepared Fe3O4 means that the GQDs loading is solely presented onto the Fe3O4 surface through the APTES linkage and has little influence on Fe3O4 core (Fig. S4B, Supplementary data).

Fig. 2C shows the magnetic hysteresis loop of Fe3O4-based nanomaterials by SQUID. It is clear that no hysteresis of field-dependent magnetization is observed at 300 K, indicating that all the Fe3O4-based nanomaterials can exhibit superparamagnetic behaviours. The saturation magnetization decreases from 73 emu g−1 for as-prepared Fe3O4 to 39 emu g−1 for APTES-coated Fe3O4 and then to 31 emu g−1 for GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites. When normalized to the unit mass of Fe3O4 based on the TGA data, the saturation magnetizations are 75.8, 43.2 and 35.3 emu g−1 for as-prepared Fe3O4, APTES-Fe3O4 and GQD-ConA@Fe3O4, respectively, indicating the presence of strong layer of APTES and GQD on the surface [38].

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of as-prepared Fe3O4, APTES-coated Fe3O4 and GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 was carried out in N2 to evaluate the loading content of organic layer on the nanocomposites (Fig. 2D). The total weight losses are found to be 3.7, 9.8 and 12.1% for as-prepared Fe3O4, APTES-coated Fe3O4 and GQD-ConA@Fe3O4, respectively. The significant weight loss of as-prepared Fe3O4 at 260–370 °C is associated with the dehydroxylation of structural Fe-O [10]. For APTES-coated Fe3O4, the weight loss observed in the temperature range of 280–430 °C is mainly attributed to the decomposition of oxygenated and hydrogenated functional groups of APTES [39]. The thermogram of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 shows an additional 2.3% weight loss in comparison with that of APTES-coated Fe3O4, which can be assigned as the attached amounts of GQD-ConA in nanocomposites. It is noteworthy that the TGA results are obtained in N2, which mean that only partial carbon materials would be decomposed. From the TGA weight loss percentage calculation, we can estimate that the approximate GQD-ConA loading is around 10% of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [40]. As the weight ratio between GQDs and ConA is maintained at 2:1 during the formation of GQD-ConA, it is expected that the loading of ConA in the GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites in cancerous cells is less than 5 wt % of its total weight, which corresponds to 2.86 mg kg−1 by assuming the cell weight is 3.5 × 10−12 g cell−1. Several studies have depicted that ConA may have toxic effect on liver cells when the dosage is in the range of 7.5–20 mg kg−1 [41,42]. This result indicates that the applied concentration of ConA in this study has no adverse effect on cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific detection of cancer cells by GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemically sensing ability of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites for specific detection of cancer cells was examined on Pt electrode. Fig. 3 A displays the CV curves of Pt||GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 electrodes at various concentrations of cancerous HeLa cells in the potential window of −0.1 V to 0.6 V at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The CV curve of bare Pt||GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 shows the strong oxidation and reduction peaks of Fe(CN)6 3− /4- at 0.27 and 0.16 V, respectively. The Pt||GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 electrode exhibits significant decreases in peak current intensities without obvious change in peak position after increasing the HeLa cell concentration from 1 × 102 to 1 × 105 cells mL−1, presumably due to fact that the large and hindered HeLa cells gradually cover the conducting GQDs surface and resist the electron transfer process of Fe(CN)6 3− /4- in the solution, and subsequently decreases peak intensities [43]. However, the diminution of peak intensity of cancerous HeLa cells is significantly prominent in comparison with the noncancerous endothelial cells (Fig. S5A, Supplementary data). It is interesting to note that the excellent linearity on oxidation (R2 = 0.997) as well as reduction peak (R2 = 0.986) intensity with respect to HeLa cell concentration in the range of 5 × 102 to 1 × 105 cells mL−1 is also obtained (Fig. 3B), clearly showing that ConA in the nanocomposites can effectively recognize and attach on the glycogeneted environment of cancer cells. Since almost all the cancer cells exhibit glycogenated behavior because of the excess glucose requirement for rapid cell division, the developed sensor can be used for detection of any type of cancer cells.

To confirm the hypothesis, the developed sensing electrode was also applied for the detection of another cancerous cell, MCF-7. As shown in Figs. S5B and S5C (Supplementary data), similar type of CV curves including the calibration curve is obtained, proving that the developed GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 is a promising materials for effective detection of most cancerous cells. Fig. 3C show linear relationship of peak current and cell number for three different cells. The comparison of peak current between two cancerous cells (HeLa and MCF-7) noncancerous Endothelial cells clearly indicates that the GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 sensor is specific for cancerous cells over other noncancerous cells. The limits of detection (LODs) of oxidation and reduction peaks (ΔIox and ΔIred), calculated from the 3 times of standard deviation of lowest concentration, are 273 and 496 cells mL−1, respectively. Table 1 shows the comparison of analytical performance of our developed sensors with the recently published reports on electrochemical cell detection. Among the non-antibody and aptamer based immuno-sensors, the GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 sensor shows the excellent linearity with a wide range of cell concentration and superior detection limit. In addition, the sensor can be applied to detect most of the cancer cells instead of any specific cancer cell line because of the usage of ConA in the nanocomposites. The strong interaction between highly glycolated cell surface of cancerous cells with ConA makes the Pt||GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 electrode a promising sensing element with high specificity, resulting in the highly sensitive detection of cancerous cells at extreme low cell concentration. To find out the role of ConA in the sensor, the nanocomposite without ConA attachment is used for drug delivery. As expected, the GQD-ConA nanocarrier exhibits little ability on recognition of the cell surface due to the non-existence interaction of GQD-Fe3O4 with cell surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Dox loaded GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites as nanocarriers
ce:section.ce:para: The possible application of using GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites as drug delivery nanocarriers was further evaluated. Doxorubicin (Dox) was selected as the standard drug molecule due to its high fluorescence property and well established characterisations. Fig. 4 A shows the dispersivity of Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 related nanomaterials. The mean hydrodynamic diameters of GQDs and as-prepared Fe3O4 are 47 and 352 nm, respectively. The coating of APTES and GQD-ConA increases the dispersity of nanomaterials in aqueous phase [56]. Therefore, the mean hydrodynamic diameters of APTES-Fe3O4 and GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 decrease to 147 and 155 nm, respectively, confirming the reduced aggregation of nanocarriers. The addition of Dox into the GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers slightly increases the diameter to 163 nm, which indicates that the loading of Dox on GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 would not cause the obvious aggregation in PBS. It is noted that the developed nanocarrier is composed of three characteristic features for targeting the cancerous cells, i.e. to accumulate the drug-containing nanocarriers in the targeted area by magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles; to concentrate the nanocarriers in cancerous cells in the presence of ConA by alterations of glycoproteins and glycolipids by glycosylation [57], and the pH-triggered mechanism in cancer cells to deliver Dox [4,6]. Therefore, the homogeneous and well-dispersed nanocarriers would exhibit an excellent loading ability for Dox via strong π-π stacking interactions. Unlike the other nanotubes or layered nanosheets which load drugs on their surfaces, GQDs allow cargo loading of Dox onto both the exposed surfaces and edges.

The optical property of Dox-loaded GQDs was evaluated by using fluorescence measurements. Fig. 4B shows the change in fluorescence intensity of Dox-loaded GQDs at 460 nm after excitation at 365 nm. The titration with Dox quenches the fluorescence intensity of GQDs because of the stacking of Dox on the surface of GQDs [25]. In addition, the result shows the encapsulation of 0–3 μg mL−1 Dox over 20 μg mL−1 of GQDs and 2 μg mL−1 Dox exhibits the saturation quenching effect. Therefore, the added amount of 2 μg mL−1 Dox is chosen for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dox release profile of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 depicts the release profiles of Dox in dialysis chambers from the Dox loaded GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers at pH values of 6 and 7.4. The pHs 6.0 and 7.4 of the dialysate are chosen to mimic the cancerous acidic microenvironment and normal physiologic conditions, respectively [58], while the pH of Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers inside the dialysis bag is always maintained at pH 7.4. As shown in Fig. 5A and B, the dialysis bag is fixed exactly in the middle of separator with strong adhesion in the dialysis chamber. A magnet of 5 T is placed on one side of the chamber to monitor the release of Dox in the presence of magnetic field. After regular time intervals, 1 mL of dialysate of each chamber was sampled for fluorescence measurement while 1 mL of fresh buffer was supplemented to each chamber for volume compensation. As shown in Fig. 5C, the cumulative release of Dox from 2 μg mL−1 Dox-loaded GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers is 28% and 23% at pH 6 and 7.4, respectively, in the absence of magnetic field. The amount of Dox released increases to 61% and 40% at pH 6 and 7.4, respectively, when external magnetic field is applied.

It is noteworthy that the external magnetic field also has direct influence; however the variation in pH value on GQD-Dox environment plays the predominant role in Dox release from nanocarriers. The high Dox release at pH 6 is attributed to the increased hydrophilicity of protonated Dox under acidic conditions in comparison with the moderately retarded drug release at pH 7.4 [58]. In the presence of external magnetic field, however, the magnetic nanocarriers are driven closely to the magnetic induced wall of dialysis bag, resulting in the effective release of Dox to the target cells (Fig. 5A). Therefore, the external magnetic field indirectly promotes the nanocarriers accumulation and concentrate the drug amount at a particular orientation. These results clearly indicate that the as-prepared Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers can act as a promising carrier to promote the highly controlled drug release in the desired targeted location.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic effect of Fe3O4 in the nanocarriers
ce:section.ce:para: Cell imaging and MTT assay were employed to evaluate the cell selectivity and magnetic effect of 2 μg mL−1 Dox loaded 20 μg mL−1 GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers in cancerous HeLa and normal endothelial cells. Initially, the Dox loaded nanocarriers were incubated only in HeLa cells in the presence and absence of external magnetic field over 12 h. The remaining cell incubation conditions were identical and images were captured at regular time intervals to examine the time dependent drug release. As shown in Fig. 6 A and B, the accumulation of released Dox concentration under both conditions increases gradually with time. However, the intensity of Dox in the presence of magnetic field is significantly higher than that of in the absence of magnetic field, which is perfectly corroborated with the Dox release profile of dialysis chamber. Interestingly, the distribution of Dox is not only concentrated on the cell nucleus but also distributed in the cytoplasm with little distortion of the nucleus shape over 12 h of incubation, which indicates the enhanced Dox concentration in the HeLa cells in the presence of external magnetic field [5]. In addition, Fig. S6 (Supplementary data) shows the detailed time-dependent Dox release in noncancerous endothelial cells. From this comparison, the considerable role of magnetic effect in Dox delivery can be identified and further extended to its cytotoxic analysis.

The effect of magnetic field is also verified by the cytotoxicity data of cancerous HeLa and normal endothelial cells in the incubation time of 12 h. As shown in Fig. 6C. The toxicity of Dox-loaded GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers at various Dox concentrations of 0.5–2 μg mL−1 is enhanced in presence of magnetic field, clearly indicating the role of magnetic nanocarriers in the cellular uptake of Dox. The cell variability of both HeLa and endothelial cells decreases with the increase in Dox concentration from 0.5 to 2 μg mL−1. However, the cytotoxicity of Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers in HeLa cells is remarkably higher than that of normal cells in the presence of magnetic field. This result clearly indicates that the cancerous cells are more susceptible to Dox mediated cell death from the as-prepared Dox-loaded GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers compares with the normal cells.

To recognize the role of ConA in the nanocarriers, the comparative cell penetration of Dox was monitored in both cell lines. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , the Dox loaded GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers are incubated in HeLa and endothelial cells over 12 h of incubation in the presence of magnetic field. It is observed that less amount of Dox accumulates onto the normal endothelial cells under the identical conditions when compares with that of HeLa cells (Fig. 7A), clearly signifying the specificity of nanocarriers to cancerous cells. This can be attributed to the fact that excellent carbohydrate binding ability of ConA can effectively attach on the surface of cancerous cell and subsequently enhance the cellular uptake of Dox by receptor mediated endocytosis. Due to the presence of higher amount of altered glycoproteins and glycopeptides on the surface of cancer cells, ConA can attach to the cancer cell surface directly by using lectin (ConA) to probe the glycoproteins or glycopeptides, which could accurately reflect the cancer situation in vitro [55]. To clarify this phenomenon, we have synthesized a parallel GQD-Fe3O4 nanocarriers without adding ConA. 2 μg mL−1 Dox loaded GQD@Fe3O4 nanocarriers were incubated for 12 h in both the cell lines and Dox uptake was observed by the cell images (Fig. 7B). It is clear from the images that the intensity of Dox is almost similar in cancerous HeLa and normal endothelial cell lines, presumably attributed to the lack of specificity induced by ConA on HeLa cells.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers at various Dox loadings of 0.5, 1 and 2 μg mL−1 against HeLa cells for 12 h was examined by MTT assay (Fig. 7C). GQDs and Fe3O4-GQDs do not show significant cytotoxicity, and the cell viability remains higher than 90% [59]. In contrast, the Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers show significant cytotoxicity with the increase in Dox concentration and the nanocarriers show the highest toxicity at 2 μg mL−1 Dox with cell viability of 46.5% after 12 h of incubation, which is similar to the cell viability of free Dox (45.1%) at the same concentration. It is noteworthy that the effective Dox released from the Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers is only 61% of the total loading (from Dox release profile in Fig. 5C), which means that the toxic effect of Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers is more pronounced compared with that of free Dox at the same concentration. To confirm the role of ConA in the nanocarriers, cytotoxicity of 2 μg mL−1 Dox loaded GQD@Fe3O4 is compared with the Dox-GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers. From Fig. 7D, it is clearly observed that the ConA loaded nanocarriers not only show pronounced Dox-mediated cell death effect in cancer cells but also exhibit excellent specificity toward HeLa cells over normal cells, whereas the specificity is almost diminished in the case of GQD@Fe3O4 nanocarriers in the absence of ConA attachment.

From the above results, the roles of ConA and Fe3O4 can be elucidated in a pH mediated drug delivery of GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocarriers. The nanocarriers can accumulate in the magnetic field-induced environment due to the incorporation of Fe3O4 into the nanocarriers. Although the magnetic force does not have any direct influence on the drug release, it can drag and concentrate the nanocarriers toward target directed by the external magnetic field. Dox released from the nanocarriers is solely dependent on pH variation where the GQDs play the crucial role. In addition, incorporation of ConA is established by its specialized function toward cancer cells over normal cells, which have been unquestionably displayed from the results of fluorescence cell images as well as MTT assay shown in this study.

In this study, the GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 nanocomposites have been successfully fabricated for exploring their multifunctional applications in electrochemical cancer cells detection as well as pH responsive drug delivery. The homogenously distributed GQDs assembled with ConA is covalently attached with APTES functionalized Fe3O4 to form the stable nanocomposites. While coated on a Pt electrode, the nanocomposites can effectively detect HeLa cells by electrochemical cyclic voltammetric process in the concentration range of 5 × 102 to 1 × 105 cells mL−1 with excellent linearity. The Dox release profile is optimized by the dialysis chamber to elucidate the effect of pH and external magnetic field on drug delivery. In addition, from the cell imaging studies and MTT assays, it can be concluded that the specificity towards cancerous cells has been successfully achieved by the ConA attachment, making the nanocarriers as unique and promising materials for pH-sensitive drug delivery. The biocompatibility, excellent electrochemical and cellular uptake properties, and highly targeted intracellular release of Dox in cancer cells can serve as the basis to build up the platform for future sensitive cancer cell detection and drug-controlled release.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan for financial support under grant Nos. 104-2221-E-009-020-MY3 and 105-2113-M-009-023-MY3. ADC is acknowledged to the MOST, Taiwan for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.04.029.
